patient.communicationStyle: Downplays bladder symptoms unless directly asked. Frustrated with worsening pain. May not volunteer neurologic symptoms without prompting.
patient.emotionalState: Frustrated with pain, embarrassed about bladder issues

responses.opening: I threw my back out a few days ago lifting something heavy at work. I've had back problems before, but this time it's different. The pain is going down both my legs now, and it's not getting better with Advil like it usually does.
responses.severity: It's bad — maybe 8 out of 10. The worst part is the leg pain. Both legs feel heavy and tingly, especially down the back.
responses.location: Low back, right in the center. And it shoots down both legs, all the way to my feet.
responses.onset: Started about 4 days ago when I was lifting at work. It was just low back pain at first, but the leg stuff started about 2 days ago and it's getting worse.
responses.character: The back pain is a deep ache. The leg pain is more like a burning, shooting, electric feeling.
responses.worse: Coughing, sneezing, straining — all make it worse. Sitting for a while makes the leg stuff worse too.
responses.better: Lying on my side helps a little with the back, but the leg numbness doesn't change with position.
responses.radiation: Both legs. All the way down the back of my legs to my feet. My inner thighs feel numb too — like a saddle area.

chiefComplaint.onset: Acute onset 4 days ago with heavy lifting, progressive leg symptoms over 48 hours
chiefComplaint.timing: Progressive — initially just back pain, now bilateral radiculopathy and numbness

present.name: bilateral leg numbness/tingling
present.patientSays: Both legs feel tingly and heavy. It's down the back of both legs to my feet.

present.name: saddle anesthesia
present.patientSays: Now that you mention it, yeah — the area around my... bottom... feels kind of numb. Like when your foot falls asleep. I noticed it when I use the toilet.

present.name: urinary retention/overflow
present.patientSays: I've been having trouble peeing. I feel like I have to go, but it takes a while to start and it doesn't feel like I'm emptying all the way. I've been dribbling a little.

present.name: constipation/decreased rectal tone
present.patientSays: Haven't had a bowel movement in two days. That's unusual for me.

present.name: bilateral leg weakness
present.patientSays: My legs feel weak. I've been tripping a little — my feet aren't lifting right.

pmh: Chronic low back pain (multiple episodes over 10 years)
Known L4-L5 disc bulge (MRI 2 years ago, managed conservatively)

medications.name: Ibuprofen
medications.patientSays: I've been taking 3 or 4 Advil at a time.

medications.name: Cyclobenzaprine
medications.patientSays: I have some muscle relaxers from my last flare-up.

socialHistory.livingSituation: Lives with wife and two children

physicalExam.general: Middle-aged adult in obvious discomfort, shifting positions frequently, unable to sit comfortably
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs
physicalExam.abdominal: Soft, non-tender. Palpable suprapubic fullness (distended bladder).
physicalExam.musculoskeletal: Midline lumbar tenderness at L4-S1. Paraspinal muscle spasm bilaterally. Severely limited forward flexion.

neurologic.motor: Bilateral ankle dorsiflexion weakness (4/5 bilaterally). EHL weakness (3/5 bilateral). Knee extension 5/5 bilaterally. Bilateral foot drop gait noted.
neurologic.sensory: Decreased sensation in saddle distribution (S2-S4 dermatomes — perineum, medial buttocks). Decreased sensation in L5-S1 distributions bilaterally (lateral leg, dorsal foot).
neurologic.reflexes: Ankle reflexes absent bilaterally. Knee reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Absent bulbocavernosus reflex.
neurologic.straightLegRaise: Positive bilaterally at 30 degrees with reproduction of radiating pain.
neurologic.rectalExam: Decreased rectal tone. Decreased perianal sensation ('wink' reflex diminished).

imaging.findings: Large central L4-L5 disc extrusion with inferior migration, causing severe compression of the cauda equina. Thecal sac effacement >75%. Multiple nerve roots displaced and compressed. No epidural abscess or mass lesion.
imaging.impression: Large L4-L5 central disc herniation with cauda equina compression. EMERGENT SURGICAL CONSULTATION RECOMMENDED.

onCorrectTreatment.symptomsTrend: Surgical decompression within 24-48 hours. Bladder function begins recovering 1-2 weeks post-op. Some residual leg numbness may persist.
onCorrectTreatment.narrative: Neurosurgery consulted emergently. Foley catheter placed (drains 700mL). IV dexamethasone started. Taken to OR within 12 hours for emergent laminectomy and discectomy. Bladder function and motor strength gradually improve post-operatively.

onDelayedTreatment.narrative: Delay beyond 48 hours significantly worsens prognosis for bladder recovery. Patient develops complete urinary retention requiring long-term catheterization.

keyLearningPoints: Cauda equina red flags: bilateral radiculopathy, saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, decreased rectal tone
This is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY — decompression within 24-48 hours for best outcomes
Bladder dysfunction (retention with overflow) is the most prognostically important symptom
Post-void residual >200mL is highly concerning in this context
MRI is the diagnostic modality of choice — order emergently, not routinely
Never dismiss new bilateral leg symptoms or bladder complaints in a back pain patient
mustNotMiss: Epidural abscess (fever, IVDU, recent procedure + neurologic deficits)
Spinal cord compression from metastatic disease (weight loss, known cancer, night pain)
Abdominal aortic aneurysm (pulsatile mass, tearing pain, hemodynamic instability)
commonPitfalls: Assuming it's 'just another back strain' without a full neuro exam
Not checking post-void residual or performing rectal exam
Not asking about saddle numbness — patients often don't volunteer this
Sending patient home with pain meds and 'follow up with ortho'
Ordering routine (non-emergent) MRI instead of emergent imaging

expertDifferential.why: Bilateral radiculopathy + saddle anesthesia + urinary retention + decreased rectal tone = classic presentation
Bilateral symptoms from multilevel disc disease, but bladder/bowel symptoms push toward CES
Similar presentation but typically more symmetric and UMN signs may be present
Progressive neuro deficits, but afebrile with normal inflammatory markers
No cancer history, no weight loss, no night pain

teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutBladder: Critical question. Urinary retention is the most important prognostic indicator in cauda equina syndrome.
teachingHooks.ifMissesSaddleAnesthesia: The patient won't volunteer this. You must ask specifically: 'Have you noticed any numbness around your bottom, inner thighs, or groin area?'
teachingHooks.ifSkipsRectalExam: Rectal tone and perianal sensation are essential in suspected cauda equina. Absent bulbocavernosus reflex + decreased tone is a surgical emergency finding.
teachingHooks.ifOrdersXrayInstead: X-rays don't show soft tissue. This patient needs emergent MRI. Every hour of delay can impact neurologic recovery.
teachingHooks.ifDischargesPatient: This patient cannot be discharged. Cauda equina syndrome is a surgical emergency. Delayed decompression leads to permanent deficits.

patient.communicationStyle: Writhing, unable to get comfortable. Short answers due to pain. May be demanding about pain medication.
patient.emotionalState: Severe distress, impatient, wants pain relief above all else

responses.opening: Oh god, doc, you gotta help me. This pain hit me about 3 hours ago — it's in my left side, like someone's stabbing me in the kidney. It comes in waves and it's the worst pain I've ever had. I can't sit still.
responses.severity: 10 out of 10 when it hits. Maybe a 6 between the waves. I've broken bones and this is worse.
responses.location: Left flank, right here. It goes around to my lower belly and down toward my groin.
responses.onset: Started out of nowhere about 3 hours ago. I was just sitting at home and boom — this wave of pain hit.
responses.character: It comes in waves. Crampy, sharp, deep pain. Feels like something's trying to push through inside me.
responses.worse: Nothing makes it worse — it just comes in waves on its own.
responses.better: Nothing helps. I've tried every position. I took some Tylenol and it did nothing.

chiefComplaint.duration: Colicky — waves of 1-2 minutes, with 5-10 minute partial relief
relieving: unable to find comfortable position — writhing

present.name: nausea and vomiting
present.patientSays: I threw up twice at home. I'm still nauseous.

present.name: hematuria
present.patientSays: My urine looked pink the last time I went. That's never happened before.

present.name: urinary urgency/frequency
present.patientSays: I feel like I have to pee all the time but only a little comes out.

present.name: restlessness
present.patientSays: I can't lie still. I've been pacing and rolling around trying to find a comfortable position.

socialHistory.occupation: Construction foreman — works outdoors in heat
socialHistory.diet: High protein, doesn't drink much water — mostly soda and beer

physicalExam.general: Adult in severe distress, writhing on gurney, unable to find comfortable position. Diaphoretic.
physicalExam.abdominal: Soft, mild LLQ tenderness. No rebound or guarding. No pulsatile mass. Bowel sounds present.
physicalExam.back: Severe left CVA tenderness on percussion
physicalExam.neurologic: Alert but distressed, no focal deficits
physicalExam.genitourinary: No testicular tenderness or swelling (if male). No hernias.

labs.value: Large blood, 50-100 RBC/hpf, no WBC, no nitrites, no bacteria
labs.note: Hematuria without pyuria — classic for stone
Elevated — consistent with gout history, possible uric acid stone

imaging.findings: 6mm obstructing stone at the left ureterovesical junction. Moderate left hydroureteronephrosis. No perinephric stranding suggesting infection. No other stones identified.
imaging.impression: 6mm obstructing left distal ureteral stone with moderate hydronephrosis. No evidence of infection.

keyLearningPoints: Renal colic: colicky flank pain + hematuria + restlessness is the classic triad
Patients with renal colic CANNOT get comfortable — this distinguishes from peritoneal pain (lie still)
Non-contrast CT is the gold standard for stone diagnosis
Stones <5mm usually pass spontaneously; 5-10mm may need intervention; >10mm usually require intervention
Must rule out complicated stone: infection (pyonephrosis) = urology emergency
mustNotMiss: Ruptured AAA (older patient, pulsatile mass, hypotension — can mimic renal colic)
Renal infarction (atrial fibrillation + flank pain + hematuria)

expertDifferential.why: Classic colicky flank pain + hematuria + CVA tenderness + restlessness + risk factors
Flank pain and CVA tenderness, but no fever, no pyuria
Flank location, but colicky nature and hematuria point to stone
Must consider in older patients, but no pulsatile mass and patient is younger
LLQ pain, but no peritoneal signs, UA findings point to renal
